common.copyError: Couldn't copy — select and copy manually

hero.title: Everything your AI agent needs
hero.lead: A toolkit for different AI agents that build development pipelines for quality products.

problem.title: What goes wrong without a process

items.title: Chaos on large tasks
items.body: Without a plan, the agent does everything at once in random order. Halfway through it loses track of what it started.

items.title: Forgets context
items.body: Nothing carries over between sessions. Every time you re-explain the stack, conventions, the folders to leave alone.

items.title: Breaks neighboring code
items.body: Fixing one thing nicks three others. You find out when the test suite goes red.

items.title: Builds the wrong thing
items.body: It misreads the task, writes 500 lines, and the result is off-target. Revert and start over.

solution.title: Three commands that turn an agent into an executor
solution.lead: Not a plugin, not a service — just files in your project that the agent reads on session start and follows.

items.title: Knows your stack
items.body: Describe the project once in .aikit/manifest.yaml — the agent knows the language, framework, build/test commands, forbidden paths, your conventions.

items.title: Breaks work into verifiable steps
items.body: Plan first — artifacts, steps, definition of done. Then code. One commit per step so you can roll back anything.

items.title: Stops for confirmation
items.body: After the plan, after risky steps, before commits. You decide what happens next — the agent doesn't run off on its own.

steps.title: You describe what you need
steps.body: One sentence in chat: "add CSV export". The agent reads the project itself and proposes a plan.

steps.title: You review the plan
steps.body: You see the list of steps and artifacts. Approve, tweak, or cancel.

steps.title: The agent executes step by step
steps.body: One step, one commit. At checkpoints it stops and shows you the result.

steps.title: You confirm and move on
steps.body: At each step: continue, correct, or stop. You stay in control.

runners.title: One process — five AI agents
runners.lead: The same commands work everywhere. Switching from Cursor to Claude Code? The manifest and process stay the same.

cta.title: Want to try it?
cta.body: Setup takes a minute: open the agent in your project, paste the prompt, answer two questions.

hero.title: Set up in a minute
hero.lead: Copy the full prompt, paste it into Claude Code or OpenCode inside your project, answer two questions. The agent handles the rest.

quickSetup.title: Setup prompt
quickSetup.subtitle: Copy → paste into your AI agent's chat → answer two questions
steps: The agent asks which language to use for setup (English / Russian).
Scans your project and asks which AI runner and model family to use.
Shows a draft .aikit/manifest.yaml and waits for your approval.
Downloads kit-setup, verifies the manifest, generates files, commits the result.

files.title: What lands in your project
files.lead: One manifest and a set of files for the chosen runner. Your existing source code is left alone.

tree.note: single YAML for the whole project

commands.title: Day-to-day work
commands.lead: The same commands across every runner. Each one runs in a fresh chat session.

items.name: /kit
items.subtitle: Planning
items.body: Describe the task in one sentence — the agent studies the code, assesses risk, drafts a plan with artifacts, steps, and definition-of-done. It stops for your approval.

items.name: /kit-do
items.subtitle: Execution
items.body: Walks through the approved plan step by step, one commit per step, with verification. At checkpoints it shows what it built and waits for your "go".

items.name: /kit-fix
items.subtitle: Targeted fix
items.body: When a bug surfaces, it gets its own session with clean context. Triage → debug → fix → re-verify. Keeps the main plan uncluttered.

scenarios.title: What this looks like in practice

items.title: New feature
items.body: /kit "add CSV export for orders". The agent reads the modules, drafts a 5-step plan, waits for approval, then /kit-do walks through them.

items.title: Fix a bug
items.body: /kit-fix "the save button stops responding on the second click". Isolated session, doesn't disturb the current plan.

items.title: Change stack or bump dependencies
items.body: Edit stack.profiles in the manifest (e.g. add nextjs), run kit-setup generate — every config is re-rendered.

items.title: Lock the agent out of legacy/
items.body: Add legacy/ to policies.forbidden_patterns — the rule lands consistently in every generated file.

items.title: Add a new sub-agent
items.body: Describe the role in agents[] (e.g. review-agent), run generate — a new sub-agent file appears under .claude/agents/ or .opencode/agents/.

cli.title: kit-setup CLI
cli.lead: If you'd rather not hand setup to an agent, you can drive everything yourself. The binary is on the releases page — one file per platform.

subcommands.body: Validates the manifest. Returns JSON {"valid": bool, "errors": [...]}. Use it in an edit-then-verify loop.
Renders files. Returns JSON {"ok": bool, "generated": [...]}. Idempotent — overwrites its own files cleanly.
Catalog of bundled templates: profiles, adapters, dialects, commands, skills.

exit.body: Usage, manifest load, parse, or I/O failure.

hero.title: AI Kit in Claude Code
hero.lead: What lands in .claude/, how it leans on Claude Code features — sub-agents, skills, hooks — and how the Anthropic dialect differs from generic.

files.title: What gets written to your project
files.lead: Running kit-setup generate with render_targets: [claude-code] writes exactly these files. Existing source code is untouched.

rows.purpose: Main instruction file: project conventions, forbidden patterns, the body of the Main agent (it drives the loop). Claude Code reads this in every session.
Sub-agents — isolated one-shot contexts that Main dispatches via the Task tool. Default: Researcher.
Slash commands /kit, /kit-do, /kit-fix. Each file's body is the prompt Claude Code substitutes when invoked.

Skills with auto-triggers driven by their descriptions. Defaults: summary-format (block shapes), agent-failure-modes (diff-review patterns), verify-by-hand-tiers (per-tier Human-required rules), aikit-plan-artifact (plan-file format).
User-facing helper prompts. Not auto-invoked — paste them into chat manually when needed.
Permissions, Bash allowlist, MCP server config. Ships with sensible defaults.
Optional. Auto-checks on PreToolUse / PostToolUse / Stop. E.g. block Edit outside permitted directories.

features.title: What AI Kit leverages in this environment

items.title: Sub-agents via the Task tool
items.body: Each file in .claude/agents/ is an isolated one-shot context. The Main agent dispatches them with the built-in Task tool when it needs to keep heavy research out of the main conversation. They return one message — a compact digest.

items.title: Auto-triggered skills
items.body: Each SKILL.md has a description. Claude Code matches user intent against it and loads the skill automatically. AI Kit uses this to guarantee the format of planning blocks.

items.title: Hooks for guardrails
items.body: PreToolUse can block a tool call (e.g. Bash with rm -rf), PostToolUse can add a check after Edit, Stop can do a final validation. All hooks are plain .mjs files inside the project.

items.title: Permissions and MCP in settings.json
items.body: Bash allowlist, explicit write permissions, MCP server configuration. One file, lives with the project — reviewable like any other code.

dialect.title: How prompts differ from generic
dialect.lead: Wrappers in dialects/anthropic/ are written for Claude's quirks: they refer to native tool APIs (Task, Glob, Grep), use structured XML tags for long context, and read more like instructions to a colleague than to a machine. The generic dialect for non-Anthropic models is more directive and skips Claude-specific affordances.

hero.title: AI Kit in other agents
hero.lead: Same three commands, same manifest — different files per runner. Here's what gets generated and how each environment differs.

opencode.title: OpenCode
opencode.lead: Open-source counterpart to Claude Code with sub-agents and slash commands. No hooks — TypeScript plugins instead.

rows.purpose: Main instruction file, the AGENTS.md analog of CLAUDE.md.
Sub-agents, each with their own permission set.
Skills in the same format as Claude Code.
Plugins in place of hooks. Same effect — guardrails and validation — via TypeScript modules.

qwen.title: Qwen Code
qwen.lead: Mirrors the OpenCode shape but everything lives under .qwen/. The main instruction file is pulled in via contextFileName in settings.json.

rows.purpose: Main instruction file. Path is declared in settings.json via contextFileName.

items.name: Cursor
items.body: No sub-agents, no slash commands. AI Kit renders the constitution as a set of .cursor/rules/*.mdc files with alwaysApply: true, plus .cursor/mcp.json. Useful so the same rules apply automatically across the team.

items.name: Aider
items.body: No sub-agents and no slash commands. AI Kit writes CONVENTIONS.md (Aider loads it automatically), .aider.conf.yml for the global model setting, and user prompts under .aider/prompts/.

dialect.title: Prompts for non-Anthropic models
dialect.lead: When provider != anthropic, wrappers come from dialects/generic/. More directive wording, no Anthropic-specific affordances (Task tool, long XML context), denser text — short context windows in some models force tighter prose. qwen and deepseek have their own subdirectories with minor tweaks.
